stem: Philip Pinel is historically credited with unchaining mentally ill patients. Who specifically assisted him in his reforms by classifying patients according to their degree of mental disturbance?
options: Benjamin Rush, who also played a vital role in the early hospital as a physician reformer
Jean Baptiste Pussin, who played a vital role in the early hospital as Nursing Superintendent
Dorothea Dix, who campaigned for the registration of state funds to build mental hospitals

stem: Benjamin Rush is referred to as the 'Father of American Psychiatry.' Which of the following BEST describes his specific theoretical contribution to the understanding of mental illness?
options: He believed mental illness was a disease of the mind and not a 'passion of demon,' and classified mental illnesses into groups based on their social and environmental causes

He identified mental illness as being caused by disruption of the blood circulation and treated them with devices that increase blood supply to the brain, writing the first psychiatric textbooks in the United States in 1812
He established 32 state asylums and fought for the unchaining of the mentally ill, campaigning in 1775 for the state to build a separate mental ward

stem: Florence Nightingale (1820-1910) made specific contributions to psychiatric nursing reform. Which of the following MOST accurately reflects her contribution as described in the text?
options: She opened 32 states Asylums and led the crusade for the unchaining of mentally ill patients in America and Europe

She fought for humane treatment of the mentally ill, encouraged Linda Richard to open Boston College in 1882, and encouraged proper patient care including better food, light, ventilation, and use of drugs to chemically restrain violent patients
She published the first psychiatric textbooks in the United States and campaigned in 1775 for building a separate mental ward for patients

stem: Dorothea Dix (1802-1887) is known for a specific legislative achievement in 1865. What was this achievement?
options: She campaigned in 1865 for the registration of a state fund to build and support mental hospitals, resulting in 62 public hospitals built exclusively for the care of the mentally ill in the USA
She founded the first school of psychiatric nursing in 1865 and registered 32 asylums under federal protection for the humane treatment of the mentally ill
She fought for the passage of the Mental Health Act in 1865, which mandated that all public hospitals allocate 20% of their beds to psychiatric patients

stem: Professor Thomas Adeoye Lambo was the most eminent African Psychiatrist of his generation. Which of the following specifically describes his PIONEERING clinical discovery?
options: He discovered that schizophrenic patients recover quickly, do not lack chronicity, and have good prognosis attributed to favourable social and environmental factors inherent in the African community
He established the first psychiatric hospital in Eastern Nigeria immediately after the civil war and campaigned for the training of community psychiatric nurses
He was the first to discover that affective psychoses and psycho-neurosis were more prevalent than schizophrenia among African patients in WHO pilot studies

stem: Dr. G.E.A. Izuora made specific contributions to psychiatric nursing in Nigeria. Which of the following is MOST accurately attributed to him?
options: He was the first indigenous principal of school of psychiatric nursing Aro and contributed to the development of psychiatric nursing training in Nigeria

He was the first psychiatrist in Eastern Nigeria after the civil war, helped establish the psychiatric hospital in Enugu, established the psychiatric school of Nursing in Enugu in 1972, and campaigned for the training of doctors in psychiatry
He was born in Abeokuta, Ogun State and was formerly Assistant Director General of the World Health Organization (WHO)

stem: Mr. D.A. Babayode is notable in Nigerian psychiatric nursing history for which SPECIFIC distinction?
options: He was the first psychiatrist in Eastern Nigeria and died in 1984 after campaigning for community psychiatric nurses
He was the first indigenous principal of school of psychiatric nursing Aro and hails from Ipetimode in Osun State, contributing to the development of psychiatric nursing training in Nigeria
He was the most eminent African Psychiatrist confirmed by WHO after a pilot study in schizophrenia and was born in Abeokuta

stem: During the Demonological Period, the treatment of mentally ill patients was placed in whose hands, and what measures were taken when treatment brought no improvement?
options: Treatment was administered by physicians who used bleeding, purging, and mechanical restraints; when these failed, the patient was transferred to asylums

Treatment was in the hands of the priest who had the power to perform exorcism; if treatment brought no improvement, extreme measures were taken including punishing by flogging, burning, and even stoning them to death
Treatment was administered by temple physicians who recommended occupational therapy, music, and wine; failure led to institutionalisation in dedicated Greek and Roman temples

stem: Hippocrates (460-377 BC) made a foundational contribution to understanding mental disorders. Which statement BEST reflects his view that distinguished him from the Demonological era?
options: Hippocrates agreed that mental illness was caused by evil spirits but argued that physicians rather than priests should perform the exorcism
Hippocrates rejected the idea of demonology and maintained that mental disorders were the result of a disturbance in the balance of body fluid and brain
Hippocrates introduced the concept of the unconscious mind and argued that mental illness was caused by fear of the future or past

stem: Asclepiades (124 BC) is described as a pioneer in humane treatment. Which TWO specific observations did he make that guided his treatment approach?
options: He observed that frenzy patients have hallucinations while maniacs have delusions; he recommended occupational therapy, music, and wine and discouraged keeping patients in the dark
He observed that frenzy patients have delusions while maniacs have hallucinations; he recommended light therapy and physical exercise and discouraged the use of mechanical restraints
He observed that manic patients respond to bleeding and purging while frenzy patients respond to isolation; he recommended separating them for individualised treatment

stem: During the Political Period, how were mentally ill patients used by the public, and what characterised their treatment in institutions?
options: The mentally ill were chained in asylums and displayed to the public for entertainment like animals in a zoo; treatment was very crude including flogging, bleeding, and cold baths
The mentally ill were placed in workhouses and used as cheap labour; they were branded as criminals and treated by early magistrates through the court system
The mentally ill were isolated in church-run monasteries and treated with prayer and fasting; those who failed to recover were publicly executed

stem: Linda Richard's MOST significant contribution to psychiatric nursing history in America was:
options: She was the first American psychiatric nurse who graduated in 1873, developed better nursing care in psychiatric hospitals, and fought for the state to build and support mental hospitals
She opened Boston College in 1882 and was trained as the first Psychiatric Nurse in the 1840s, and she is known as the 'mother of psychiatric nursing'
She opened Boston College in 1882 where progressive nursing was taught and is credited as the first Psychiatric Nurse in the 1840s

stem: The Humanitarian Period is marked by which pivotal event involving Philip Pinel and the first Parish Physician?
options: Philip Pinel was the first parish physician to order the release from chain of lunatics in Bicetre Hospital in France, and he was the first to treat insanity not as a crime but also with kindness
Philip Pinel was the first physician to introduce the concept of moral treatment and built the first dedicated institution for the mentally ill in Paris in 1791
Philip Pinel collaborated with Jean Baptiste Pussin to establish the first school of psychiatric nursing in France, marking the beginning of the humanitarian era

stem: Sigmund Freud's contribution during the Scientific Period fundamentally changed the understanding of mental illness. Which of the following MOST accurately describes his contribution?
options: Freud believed mental illness was a malfunction of the unconscious mind unknown to the patient; his work led to understanding mental illness as a modifiable abnormal behaviour
Freud introduced insulin coma therapy in 1930 and electroconvulsive therapy as successful biological treatments for mental illness in the early 20th century
Freud developed the first tranquillising drug named largactil (chlorpromazine) in the 1950s and laid the foundation for psychopharmacology in psychiatric treatment

stem: Psychiatric Nursing is defined in the text as a branch of nursing concerned with the prevention, care, and cure of mental disorders. Which THREE specific functions does Psychiatric Nursing perform for nurses?
options: It makes a nurse aware of what she is doing psychologically to a patient; it educates her on how to observe her patient; it equips her with appropriate techniques to meet the patient's psychological needs and manage the nurse-patient relationship
It trains the nurse to diagnose mental illness; it provides pharmacological knowledge for psychiatric medication; it develops her ability to perform psychotherapy independently
It teaches the nurse demonological theories of mental illness; it prepares her to manage physical restraint; it ensures she can classify mental illness according to the ICD-10 criteria

stem: Thomas Adeoye Lambo O.D.E. was born on March 29th, 1923. He was formerly the Assistant Director General of which international body, and his findings on schizophrenia were confirmed by that same body?
options: He was formerly Assistant Director General of UNICEF, and his findings were confirmed by the United Nations Commission on Human Rights after a pilot study

He was formerly Assistant Director General of the World Health Organization (WHO), and his findings on schizophrenic patients' recovery and prognosis were confirmed by WHO after a pilot study in schizophrenia
He was formerly Assistant Director General of the African Union Health Commission, and his findings were confirmed by WHO after a multinational African psychiatric survey

stem: The text states that the history of psychiatric nursing is as old as the human race. In early Christian times, what was mental illness considered to be?
options: Mental illness was considered a physical disease caused by disturbance in body fluids as described by Hippocrates and adopted by Christian physicians
Mental illness was considered to be the result of possession by demons or evil spirits, as reflected in biblical examples including Nebuchadnezzar and Saul
Mental illness was considered a divine punishment for personal sin, and treatment involved prayer and priestly intercession in dedicated religious institutions

stem: During the Political Period, the Bethlem Royal Hospital (Bedlam) in London is referenced. Which statement correctly describes its role during this era?
options: Bethlem Hospital was among the 403 institutions built during Edward II's reign, admitting patients who were treated humanely with occupational therapy
Men and women were admitted to Bethlem Hospital in London where treatment was very crude; visitors were allowed to see the inmates â similar to viewing zoo animals
Bethlem Hospital was the first institution to adopt non-restraint treatment under the influence of Pinel's humanitarian reforms during the 15th century

stem: Chlorpromazine (Largactil) was introduced in the 1950s as the first tranquillising drug. What specific property distinguished it from earlier pharmacological agents such as bromide and barbiturates?
options: Chlorpromazine induced deep sedation similar to barbiturates but with the added property of reversing psychotic symptoms through dopamine receptor blockade
Chlorpromazine was the first tranquillising drug that could calm the patient without making him unconscious, unlike earlier sedatives and stimulants
Chlorpromazine replaced insulin coma therapy as the preferred biological treatment for schizophrenia due to its lower mortality rate and ease of administration

stem: The Scientific Period introduced insulin coma therapy. When was it first used, and what did it involve?
options: Insulin coma therapy was first used in the early 1920s; large amounts of insulin were given to patients to produce ketoacidosis and a prolonged hypoglycaemic state
Insulin coma therapy was first used in the early 1930s; large amounts of insulin were given to patients to produce unconsciousness as a treatment for mental illness
Insulin coma therapy was first used in the late 1940s; small amounts of insulin were given to produce controlled hypoglycaemia, stimulating appetite and cognitive recovery

stem: Benjamin Rush campaigned in 1775 for which specific institutional change in the management of the mentally ill?
options: He campaigned in 1775 for the state to build a separate mental ward where patients could be kept in more humane conditions
He campaigned in 1775 for the passage of a federal law requiring all public hospitals to accept mentally ill patients in dedicated wings
He campaigned in 1775 for the establishment of the first school of psychiatric nursing in the United States attached to public general hospitals

stem: The concept of health used to introduce Psychiatric Nursing in the text defines health as:
options: The complete absence of disease, infirmity, and mental disturbance as determined by clinical examination and psychological testing
Not merely the absence of disease but a sense of physical, psychological, and social well-being; physical and mental health care are two sides of a coin and both are interdependent
A state of psychological balance in which an individual has full insight into their mental condition and can maintain productive social and occupational functioning

stem: Rufus (110 AD) made a brief but specific recommendation for the mentally ill. What was it?
options: Rufus recommended the use of occupational therapy and music to promote sleep and discourage violent thought patterns
Rufus recommended the use of occupational and recreational therapy to distract the thoughts of mentally ill patients
Rufus recommended the use of wine, gentle stimulation, and social activities to redirect disturbed thoughts toward productive engagement

stem: Linda Richard is described as the 'mother of psychiatric nursing.' She is known for which professional accomplishment in Illinois?
options: She opened Boston City College in Illinois with the help of Florence Nightingale, where she organised nursing services and developed better nursing care in psychiatric hospitals
She founded the first state mental hospital in Illinois with the help of Dorothea Dix, training psychiatric nurses to work with forensic patients
She established psychiatric nursing as an independent profession in Illinois by lobbying for a separate nursing licensure examination for psychiatric nurses

stem: In the Humanitarian Period, what was the significance of the concept of 'non-restraint treatment' introduced in the same hospital as Pinel?
options: Non-restraint treatment meant that mechanical restraints were replaced by chemical sedation using opium and bromide, a new era of more humane biological psychiatry
Non-restraint treatment began a new era of scientific and humane treatment; nursing superintendents in the same hospital as Pinel spread non-restraint principles to Germany and America
Non-restraint treatment was proposed by Dorothea Dix after visiting European asylums, and it replaced the punitive measures used during the Political Period

stem: Pavlov's influence during the Scientific Period contributed to understanding that mental illness was related to environmental factors. How is his contribution described in this context?
options: Pavlov's work on conditioned reflexes laid the basis for understanding that attitude spread to patients from nursing superintendents through locked and padded seclusion wards

Pavlov's work and followers laid the basis for understanding that mental illness was associated with environmental factors; his attitude that mental illness spread to patients was noted from nursing superintendents of the 19th century
Pavlov's work established that behavioural conditioning could cure mental illness if applied consistently in structured hospital environments with reward and punishment

stem: The text describes Electroconvulsive Therapy (ECT) as being introduced by which two individuals, and it is still used today for which reason?
options: ECT was introduced by Carletti and Bini in 1938 to treat mental illness and is still used today because it is a very successful treatment
ECT was introduced by Sigmund Freud and Bleuler in the early 1940s and is still used today as a first-line treatment for treatment-resistant schizophrenia
ECT was introduced by Benjamin Rush and Philip Pinel as a humane alternative to insulin coma therapy and is still used today in cases of acute mania

stem: During the Demonological Period, mental illness was attributed to which SPECIFIC causes, and the treatment was therefore placed under whose authority?
options: Mental illness was attributed to disturbance in body fluids and brain chemistry; treatment was given by physicians who used herbs and exercise
Mental illness was attributed to evil spirits or demons possessing the individual, associated with witchcraft, adultery, taboo, and offences against gods; treatment was in the hands of the priest
Mental illness was attributed to social deprivation and moral failure; treatment was administered by community elders through communal rituals and reconciliation ceremonies

stem: Hippocrates recommended several treatment modalities for mentally ill patients. Which of the following correctly identifies methods he SUGGESTED alongside those he deemed LESS DESIRABLE?
options: He suggested pleasant surroundings, exercise, proper diet, massage, and soothing baths; he considered bleeding, purging, and mechanical restraints less desirable
He suggested music therapy, wine, and occupational therapy; he considered isolation, flogging, and exorcism less desirable
He suggested prayer, fasting, and temple dedication; he considered the use of drugs and physical restraints less desirable

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A student nurse asks her lecturer why mentally ill patients in ancient times were flogged, burned, and stoned. The lecturer correctly explains that this was because:
options: During the Political Period, institutions were poorly funded and staff used punishment as the only available management tool due to lack of training

During the Demonological Period, mental illness was ascribed to demonic possession; if the exorcism performed by the priest brought no improvement, extreme measures were taken to drive out the evil spirit
During the Humanitarian Period, non-restraint treatment had not yet been implemented, so physical punishment remained the standard of care

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A nurse is evaluating care in a psychiatric facility that uses isolation rooms, chains, and no therapeutic activities. This most closely resembles which historical period of psychiatric care?
options: The Humanitarian Period â where non-restraint was partially implemented but seclusion rooms were still standard practice
The Political Period â where the mentally ill were admitted to crude institutions, given very crude treatment, and displayed like animals in a zoo
The Scientific Period â where insulin coma therapy was used alongside padded seclusion rooms in standard psychiatric hospitals

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A psychiatric nurse advocates for a patient's right to proper food, fresh air, and natural light in a Nigerian psychiatric hospital. Her advocacy most closely mirrors the principles of which historical reformer?
options: Benjamin Rush â who campaigned for blood circulation devices and mental hygiene as the basis of patient treatment
Florence Nightingale â who encouraged proper patient care including better food, light, and ventilation and also used drugs to chemically restrain violent and aggressive patients
Philip Pinel â who unchained mentally ill patients and classified them according to their degree of mental disturbance

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A community mental health team in Anambra State is conducting outreach for psychiatric patients in remote villages. The pioneer who established this kind of community psychiatric initiative in this region was:
options: Professor Thomas Adeoye Lambo, who was born in Abeokuta, Ogun State and confirmed the good prognosis of schizophrenia in African communities
Dr. G.E.A. Izuora, who established the psychiatric school of Nursing in Enugu in 1972 and campaigned for community psychiatric nurses in various parts of Anambra State
Mr. D.A. Babayode, who was the first indigenous principal of psychiatric nursing Aro and contributed to the development of psychiatric nursing training in Osun State

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A patient with schizophrenia in a rural Nigerian setting is noted to recover quickly and maintain good functioning within the family unit. A nurse cites Thomas Lambo's research. What was Lambo's specific finding that explains this?
options: Lambo found that African schizophrenic patients have a genetically distinct form of schizophrenia that is more treatment-responsive to traditional anti-psychotics

Lambo found that schizophrenic patients recover quickly, do not lack chronicity, and have good prognosis attributed to favourable social and environmental factors inherent in the African community, confirmed by WHO
Lambo found that affective psychoses and psycho-neurosis were more prevalent than schizophrenia in African communities, making schizophrenia easier to manage by exclusion

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A nursing educator is teaching about the Scientific Period. She asks: 'Which physician's work laid the foundation for understanding that attitudes of nursing staff spread to patients and affected their mental health outcomes?' The correct answer is:
options: Sigmund Freud â whose work on the unconscious mind showed that nursing attitudes affect the transference relationship with patients
Pavlov and his followers â who laid the basis for understanding that mental illness was associated with environmental factors, and nursing superintendents' attitudes spread to patients
Asclepiades â who observed that institutional environment affected patient outcomes and recommended occupational therapy to counter negative staff attitudes

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A student nurse reads that in the 15th century, mentally ill people were depicted on 'Ship of Fools' paintings. This is consistent with which period of psychiatric history and what practice?
options: This is consistent with the Demonological Period â where the mentally ill were used as subjects of religious art depicting demonic possession

This is consistent with the Political Period â where the mentally ill roamed the country side like animals and were eventually admitted to institutions where they were displayed for public entertainment
This is consistent with the Humanitarian Period â where Pinel first documented the dehumanising treatment of the mentally ill in artistic representations before advocating for reform

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A nurse is explaining the role of drugs discovered in the early 1950s in transforming psychiatric care. She correctly states that before chlorpromazine, which agents were already in use, and what was their major limitation?
options: Before chlorpromazine, opiates and cannabis were used but caused dependency and lacked specific anti-psychotic properties relevant to schizophrenia management
Before chlorpromazine, bromide, chloral hydrate, and barbiturates were discovered in the early 1950s and had the effect of calming patients, but tranquillising drugs came later
Before chlorpromazine, insulin coma therapy and ECT were the only pharmacological options, and they caused permanent sedation incompatible with therapeutic nurse-patient relationships

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A psychiatric nursing student is asked to identify who organised the first nursing services and educational programs in state mental hospitals in Illinois. The correct answer is:
options: Florence Nightingale, who directly supervised nursing in Illinois as part of her international mental health reform campaign

Linda Richard, who graduated from the New England Hospital for Women and Children in Boston in 1873 and later organised nursing services in state mental hospitals in Illinois with the help of Florence Nightingale
Dorothea Dix, who opened state asylums and staffed them with trained nursing personnel recruited from Boston College as part of her 1865 campaign

stem: Philip Pinel was assisted in his reforms by ____________, who played a vital role in the early hospital as ____________ and classified patients according to their degree of mental disturbance.

Benjamin Rush called himself the 'Father of ____________ Psychiatry.' He published the first psychiatric textbooks in the United States in ____________ and wrote that mental illness is a disease of the ____________.
acceptableAnswers: American; 1812; mind and not passion of demon

stem: Dorothea Dix opened ____________ states Asylums to care for mentally ill patients and fought for the registration of state fund to build and support mental hospitals. By her campaign in 1865, there were ____________ public hospitals built exclusively for the care of the mentally ill in the USA.
Thomas Adeoye Lambo was born on March ____________, 1923, in ____________, Ogun State. He was formerly ____________ Director General of the World Health Organization.
Dr. G.E.A. Izuora was the first psychiatrist in ____________ Nigeria, immediately after the civil war. He helped establish the psychiatric school of Nursing in ____________ in ____________.
Mr. D.A. Babayode was the first ____________ principal of school of psychiatric nursing Aro. He hails from ____________ in ____________ state.
Hippocrates (460-377 BC) rejected the idea of ____________ and maintained that mental disorders were the result of a disturbance in the balance of ____________ and ____________.

Asclepiades (124 BC) observed that while ____________ patients have hallucinations, ____________ patients have delusions. He recommended ____________ therapy and discouraged the idea of keeping patients in the dark.

The ____________ Period is the earliest period in psychiatric history, found in biblical legends, where mental illness was ascribed to evil spirits or demons and treatment was in the hands of the ____________.

Philip Pinel was the first parish physician to order the release from ____________ of lunatics in ____________ Hospital in France. He was the first to treat insanity not as a ____________ but also with kindness.
Sigmund Freud believed that mental illness was a malfunction of the ____________ mind unknown to the patient. His work led to the understanding of mental illness as a ____________ behaviour.
Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) was introduced by ____________ and ____________ in ____________ to treat mental illness and is still used today.
The first tranquillising drug named ____________ (chlorpromazine) was able to calm the patient without making him ____________, unlike earlier barbiturates and sedatives.
Linda Richard graduated from the ____________ Hospital for women and children in ____________ in 1873. She is known as the 'mother of ____________ nursing standard.'
During the Political Period, in the 16th century, visitors were allowed to see the inmates in the ____________ house as a form of ____________ for the public, similar to visiting a zoo.

Nightingale was trained as the first ____________ Nurse in the ____________, where progressive nursing was taught including better food, light, ventilation, and the use of drugs to chemically ____________ violent and aggressive patients.
The history of psychiatric nursing can be considered in four separate periods: (a) ____________ period, (b) ____________ period, (c) ____________ period, and (d) ____________ period.

Lambo discovered that schizophrenic patients recover ____________, do not lack ____________, and have ____________ prognosis, which he attributed to the favourable social and environmental factors inherent in the community.
Which of the following BEST explains why Hippocrates is considered a turning point in psychiatric history despite recommending some methods still considered harsh today?
options: Hippocrates eliminated the role of religion in mental illness treatment by introducing pharmacological herbs and dietary management
Hippocrates was the first to reject demonology and attribute mental illness to natural causes â a disturbance in body fluid and brain â representing the first scientifically-oriented explanation
Hippocrates introduced the concept of the unconscious mind, later developed by Freud, establishing a continuum of scientific psychiatric understanding from ancient Greece to the 20th century

stem: Benjamin Rush treated mental illness with devices that increase blood supply to the brain. What was the underlying theory that led him to prescribe this intervention?
options: He believed mental illness was caused by disruption of blood circulation and treated it by disruption of the blood circulation and treating them with devices to increase blood supply to the brain
He believed mental illness was caused by excessive blood supply to the brain and prescribed bloodletting to reduce cerebral congestion
He believed mental illness was caused by ventricular obstruction and used hydrostatic devices to equalise intracranial pressure

stem: Which of the following pairs correctly matches the pioneer with their PRIMARY professional identity and their MAIN contribution?
options: Philip Pinel â Physician; unchained mentally ill patients and classified them by degree of mental disturbance with the help of Nursing Superintendent Jean Baptiste Pussin
Linda Richard â Physician; graduated in 1873 and opened the first state mental hospital in Illinois to provide care for the mentally ill
Dorothea Dix â Nursing Pioneer; trained as the first Psychiatric Nurse in the 1840s and encouraged occupational therapy in American asylums

stem: Orobasius (AD 320) added recommendations to those of Asclepiades, Celsius, and Rufus Adioo. Based on the text, what was the nature of this group's overall approach to the mentally ill during this period?
options: These physicians recommended chaining patients in temple precincts and using religious rituals to contain violent behaviour
These physicians were among those who cared for many of the mentally ill with great kindness by physicians in temples dedicated to Greek and Roman Gods
These physicians advocated for public institutionalisation of the mentally ill in municipal buildings funded by local Greek and Roman governments

stem: Linda Richard's contributions are described as including 'licensing of nurses' and 'development of interpersonal relationship between nurses and their patients.' These contributions place her in which specific role in psychiatric nursing's professional development?
options: She is known as the 'mother of psychiatric nursing standard,' influencing and emphasising the advancement of professional education and practice in psychiatric nursing
She is known as the first American Psychiatric Nurse who fought for states to build and support mental hospitals and to prepare staff to provide human treatment
She is known as the first Psychiatric Nurse in the 1840s who opened Boston City College in Illinois with Florence Nightingale's help

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A nursing student presents a seminar on the 'Political Period' of psychiatric care. Which of the following statements would be CORRECT to include in her presentation?
options: During the Political Period, the mentally ill were placed under the care of the Church and treated by monks with prayer, herbal medicine, and communal support

During the Political Period, mental institutions had about 403 workhouses built during the reign of Edward II; the mentally ill were displayed for entertainment like animals in a zoo and treatment was very crude
During the Political Period, Philip Pinel ordered the release of chains in Bicetre Hospital, beginning a new era of scientific and humane psychiatric treatment in Europe

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A nurse educator asks her students: 'Which Nigerian psychiatrist's work was confirmed by the WHO in a pilot study on schizophrenia, and what exactly did he discover?' The CORRECT response is:
options: Dr. G.E.A. Izuora; he discovered that schizophrenia responds poorly to treatment in Eastern Nigeria but well to community rehabilitation

Professor Thomas Adeoye Lambo; he discovered that schizophrenic patients recover quickly, do not lack chronicity, and have good prognosis attributed to favourable social and environmental community factors â confirmed by WHO
Mr. D.A. Babayode; he discovered that indigenous treatment methods for schizophrenia in Osun State produced outcomes comparable to hospital-based treatment

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A nursing preceptor is supervising a student who is rough with a psychiatric patient and dismisses the importance of food and hygiene. The preceptor invokes the principles of which historical figure?
options: Dorothea Dix â who fought to improve the treatment of mentally ill patients in America and Europe and led the crusade for the unchaining of mentally ill patients
Florence Nightingale â who encouraged proper patient care including better food, light, and ventilation and also recommended the use of drugs to chemically restrain violent and aggressive patients
Benjamin Rush â who campaigned for humane conditions and published the first psychiatric textbooks in the United States in 1812

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A psychiatric nurse in the 19th century notices that her patients deteriorate when placed in locked, padded seclusion rooms and that the nurses' negative attitudes seem to be 'contagious.' Which scientific figure's work would MOST directly explain this observation?
options: Sigmund Freud â who described how the unconscious mind of the nurse influenced the patient through counter-transference in the therapeutic relationship
Pavlov and his followers â who laid the basis for understanding that mental illness was associated with environmental factors, and that attitudes spread from nursing superintendents to patients
Carletti and Bini â who introduced electroconvulsive therapy as a humane alternative to the restrictive seclusion practices of 19th century asylums

stem: CASE SCENARIO (Fill-in): A student describes a period in psychiatric history where mentally ill patients were beaten, flogged, burned, and stoned when exorcism failed. This describes the ____________ Period, where mental illness was attributed to evil ____________ or demons, and treatment was administered by the ____________.

CASE SCENARIO (Fill-in): A historian notes that between the 15th and 18th centuries, mentally ill patients were admitted to institutions with 403 workhouses. Their treatment was very ____________, and visitors were allowed to view them like ____________ in a zoo. Men and women were admitted to ____________ Hospital in London.

CASE SCENARIO (Fill-in): A nurse explains that the first tranquillising drug â ____________ (chlorpromazine) â was different from earlier drugs like bromide, chloral hydrate, and ____________ because it could calm the patient without making him ____________.

CASE SCENARIO (Fill-in): A Nigerian nurse describes a prominent psychiatrist born on March 29th, 1923, in Abeokuta, who was formerly ____________ Director General of the WHO. He found that schizophrenic patients had ____________ prognosis and recovered quickly, and this was confirmed by the WHO after a ____________ study in schizophrenia.

CASE SCENARIO (Fill-in): A student is asked to name the reformer who opened ____________ states Asylums, fought for the improvement of the treatment of the mentally ill in America and ____________, and led the crusade for the unchaining of mentally ill patients in the USA. The answer is ____________ Dix.
